I'll move this method, and remove endpoint related code from the endpoint module.

The endpoint resolver will provide all of the metadata about an endpoint, including available signature versions. For example, `aws-cn` regions only support `s3v4` while most `aws` regions support `s3` and `s3v4`. Higher level code that consumes this endpoint metadata needs to make a choice as to how to use that metadata. In Boto3 and the CLI, we currently always pick `s3` over `s3v4`.

I'm open to suggestions on where we pick which signature version to use.

Hmm, `_absolute_path` requiring Unicode but returning bytes is definitely strange. It probably would be safest to change it to accept either Unicode or bytes but always return explicitly either Unicode or bytes. I would also assume it would be best for it to always return bytes, but that depends on its usage. At least in this case the return value is converted to bytes so returning bytes would make more sense.

The main reason this is messed up is that in Python 2 it didn't matter were Unicode or bytes accepted/returned as long as non-ASCII characters weren't involved. Making sure we accept/return correct types is probably easiest with unit tests. Haven't looked at SSHLibrary unit tests for ages, but it would be great if `_absolute_path` could be unit tested somewhat easily.

`only_for_admins` may be misleading since this is a side-effect here:
In this test it is "only for admins" because we have no active workflow and therefore the `rolemap.xml` counts, which does not grant contributors / editors.

But in production we will have a workflow, usually with `ftw.lawgiver`. In the `lawgiver.zcml` you are mapping the permission to the `edit` action group, therefore the standard behavior will be that all editors can hide the title.

Therefore I think the test name may be misleading.
How can we address that?
Should we just add a docstring to the test describing this issue?

Or is the `rolemap.xml` wrong because it does not go along with the `edit` action group? Then we should probably add editors / contributors in the `rolemap.xml` and then reconfigure the permission here so that we are explicitly building the situation in the test.

Everything here is great, by the way. The only thing I notice as sticking out to me is the fact that `token` is a protected field, and if it is not given, the modification gets sent with `$encrypted$` as its value. That's a fundamental limitation of the server, and there's literally no way we can let the user modify anything without them also giving a new token.

So the only thing I could see being done about it would be to raise an error if the token is not provided in the`modify` command. This might be complicated if the encrypted fields are specific to the type. In that case, we could probably leave it as a TODO for later.

inv_prob_per_block = 3/4

The old code made more sense to me:
```py
inv_prob_per_block = 3/4
probability_accepted = 1 - (3/4 ^ wait_blocks)
```
Where the parenthetical probability is the chance that the gas price would never be accepted in any of the wait-blocks.
